input_path: data\papers\The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Incubator Digital Twin models a laboratory thermal incubator that consists of an insulated styrofoam box, an integrated heatbed that supplies heat, a mechanical fan to distribute heat, and a set of temperature sensors (two inside the box and one outside).  The system’s actuation and control logic are hosted on a Raspberry Pi, which communicates with the heating and fan devices through low‑level driver messages.  The incubator’s operational domain is biological sample incubation, where a target temperature must be maintained regardless of the contents placed in the container.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin (DT) delivers a suite of services that support both operational monitoring and engineering optimization. 1) **Real‑time monitoring and visualization** – the DT captures sensor streams and controller telemetry, persists them in a time‑series database, and renders live charts on a dashboard. 2) **Predictive simulation** – two physics‑based temperature models (Model A with two free parameters, Model B with four) are used to forecast future thermal states under varying input conditions. 3) **Parameter calibration** – a non‑linear least‑squares solver periodically updates the free parameters of Model B by fitting simulated outputs to measured data. 4) **State estimation and anomaly detection** – a Kalman filter fuses measured temperatures with Model B predictions to estimate unmeasured heat‑bed temperature and flag deviations from the desired behavior. 5) **What‑if and optimization** – co‑simulation scenarios ingest historical or live PT data to evaluate alternative controller settings, enabling parameter tuning and robustness analysis. 6) **Self‑adaptation loop** – the DT autonomously reacts to detected behavioral changes (e.g., insertion of a new load) by orchestrating calibration experiments, re‑optimizing the controller, and updating both the model and filter, thus restoring optimal operation. 7) **User interfaces** – engineers and operators interact with the DT through the dashboard and configuration interfaces, enabling rapid inspection, experiment scheduling, and controller deployment.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin implementation relies on several key tools and technologies: the Raspberry Pi runs the low‑level controller and collects sensor data; two temperature sensors (located inside) and one external temperature sensor feed data into the system; the heating and fan components are controlled via the Raspberry Pi; digital twin software and model definitions are hosted in the public GitHub repository (https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator); simulation models (Model A with two free parameters and Model B with four free parameters) are executed to predict the internal temperature profile; and calibration algorithms update the model parameters to match measured sensor data. The overall framework integrates the data acquisition, model updating, and simulation execution within a single cohesive platform.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Synchronization between the physical incubator and the Digital Twin is coordinated through a RabbitMQ message broker. A low‑level driver on the Raspberry Pi periodically reads temperature sensor data and publishes it to RabbitMQ, where Digital Twin services consume it. Real‑time monitoring, calibration, Kalman filtering and anomaly detection run on‑the‑fly, providing near‑instantaneous feedback with sub‑second responsiveness. Batch tasks such as what‑if simulations and offline calibrations are executed on demand and do not block the real‑time loop, resulting in a three‑tier temporal architecture: (1) periodic sensor sampling, (2) sub‑second real‑time processing, and (3) asynchronous batch processing.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The architecture is a single, centralized Digital Twin instance that maps one‑to‑one with the physical incubator plant controlled by a Raspberry Pi. There is no hierarchy of multiple twin instances; coordination is achieved solely via the RabbitMQ broker exchanging messages with the controller and the low‑level driver. Each instance is responsible for sensor data ingestion, model calibration, monitoring, anomaly detection, and control adaptation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The digital twin of the incubator is organised around a modular architecture that centres on three main stages: (1) *Data ingestion* from the physical twin via two internal temperature sensors and one external temperature sensor, all connected to a Raspberry Pi controller; (2) *Model calibration* where a calibration algorithm updates the parameters of two simulation models (Model A with two free parameters and Model B with four free parameters) to best fit the measured data; and (3) *Monitoring and decision‑making* where any resulting data are fed to safety monitors that check for violations and, if necessary, trigger re‑configuration of the physical controller. The framework follows a clear feed‑forward flow from physical sensing to digital modelling, and ultimately to actuation, with no explicit replication or hierarchical structure described in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: External integration is achieved through a RabbitMQ message broker that exchanges control and telemetry messages among the Raspberry Pi controller, the low‑level driver, and the Digital Twin services. The Digital Twin publishes telemetry data to a time‑series database, which feeds a real‑time dashboard for visualization. On‑demand calibration and heat‑bed state estimation requests are also routed via the same message bus. No additional enterprise systems are directly connected; integration focuses on these open‑source components.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Digital Twin employs two behavioral simulation models for the incubator’s thermodynamics: Model A, a parameterized model with two free variables; and Model B, an extended model with four free variables that captures the heat‑bed thermal inertia. Both models are expressed as differential‑equation systems that describe the relationship between heater power, fan operation, and internal temperature. Raw sensor measurements from two internal temperature probes and one external reference probe are streamed directly from the incubator to the DT via the control bus. A calibration algorithm continuously updates the free parameters of Model A and Model B so that predictions remain aligned with the physical twin. The DT’s data layer forwards the updated model parameters and sensor streams to higher‑level monitors and dashboards; no external database or explicit versioning mechanism is described in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The incubator’s actuating hardware consists of a heatbed that supplies thermal energy to the interior, a small fan that circulates air across the heatbed and room, and a Raspberry Pi that hosts the control firmware. The Pi issues on‑off commands to the heatbed and fan in response to temperature telemetry, and all actuators are powered by a standard low‑voltage DC supply appropriate for the heatbed and fan. The control loop runs entirely on the Raspberry Pi; no external remote command interface or additional actuation hardware is specified.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Instrumentation includes three temperature sensors: two sensors embedded inside the insulated styrofoam box, and one sensor mounted on the exterior of the box to monitor ambient temperature. All sensors provide temperature readouts that are sampled at millisecond‑ to second‑intervals and transmitted over the Pi’s low‑level driver bus. The documents do not provide specific sensor models, bus protocols, or numerical accuracy/precision figures.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Calibration routines fit Model A and Model B’s free parameters to experimental temperature data collected from the physical incubator by minimizing the discrepancy between measured temperatures and model predictions. Model B, containing two additional parameters, better captures the heat‑bed thermal inertia that Model A cannot, evidencing higher fidelity. Validation is implicit in direct comparison of live sensor data to model outputs, and model adjustments are made during calibration. Sources of uncertainty include sensor noise and variability in heater performance; the documents mention no explicit uncertainty quantification or error‑correction mechanisms beyond the calibration loop.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: In the Incubator digital twin the low‑level driver running on the Raspberry Pi reads temperature data from two internal sensors and one external sensor and forwards the readings to the RabbitMQ message broker. Each reading is transmitted as a timestamped value (e.g., timestamp, inside temperature, outside temperature) over the standard AMQP TCP connection. The driver executes this data capture at a periodic interval defined by the driver loop, checks for command messages from the controller, and uploads the sensor data to the broker. Data quality assurance is performed through a basic safe‑protocol incorporated in the driver which validates sensor ranges before transmitting.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The controller receives control commands (e.g. to turn the heatbed or fan on/off) from the RabbitMQ broker. It then forwards the actuator orders to the low‑level driver via the same broker. The low‑level driver executes the commands after verifying them against a safe protocol that ensures the incubator remains in a protected state. After execution, the driver publishes the resulting control states back to the broker for telemetry. The controller implements a bang‑bang logic using the received reference temperature to decide heating or cooling actions.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The digital twin’s technical back‑end uses RabbitMQ, an open‑source AMQP messaging broker, as the central communication middleware. All components (controller, low‑level driver, DT models) publish and subscribe to RabbitMQ queues over standard TCP connections. RabbitMQ provides the necessary transport layer; the broker can be deployed on‑premises on a local network. No specific bandwidth or latency targets are detailed, but the system relies on RabbitMQ’s lightweight message passing for near‑real‑time interaction.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Both the DT software (controller, low‑level driver, and associated logic) and the RabbitMQ message broker are deployed on the same physical Raspberry Pi used for the incubator, representing an on‑premises edge deployment. All components run locally within the incubator’s network, with no external cloud or edge servers required.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Twinning was carried out through iterative modelling, calibration and deployment stages. First, two simulation models were derived – Model A with two free parameters and Model B with four free parameters – represented as differential equations governing the temperature dynamics of the heatbed and the insulated enclosure (see Sections 3 and 3.3). Calibration of the models used a least‑squares optimiser to fit the sensor data collected from the two inside temperature sensors and one outside temperature sensor. The calibrated models are then embedded into a Digital‑Twin run‑time that receives streaming telemetry from the Raspberry Pi controller and low‑level driver messages. A time‑series database stores the telemetry; a lightweight dashboard displays current temperature, heating power and heat‑bed state. A Kalman filter (reference [16]) runs in the twin to compare measured temperatures against Model B predictions, flagging anomalies and estimating unmeasured heat‑bed temperature. ‘On‑demand’ calibration is implemented by automatically launching the calibration routine described in Section 3.3 when an anomaly is detected. For foresight, what‑if simulations are executed via co‑simulations that replay historical or real‑time data through the twin to optimise controller parameters. The entire development workflow is version‑controlled in a public GitHub repository (https://github.com/INTO‑CPS‑Association/example‑incubator), where code, calibration scripts, model definitions and experiment logs are stored and continuously updated, supporting evolution of the twin across firmware upgrades and new physical configurations.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The twin provides a decision‑support pipeline that turns raw telemetry into actionable insights. A real‑time dashboard shows the current temperature inside the incubator, the commanded heating power, and the estimated internal heat‑bed temperature derived from the Kalman filter. The filter also supplies an estimation error covariance that serves as a confidence metric for the heat‑bed state estimate. Whenever the filter detects a significant deviation between measured and model‑predicted temperature (anomaly), the twin triggers an automated adaptation workflow: it schedules an experiment to expose the plant to the new condition, re‑runs calibration, updates the model and filter parameters, and runs a what‑if simulation to re‑optimise controller gains. The outcome – a new controller configuration – is then pushed back to the physical controller. This closed loop ensures that the twin continuously supplies high‑confidence estimates and controller recommendations that adapt to changes in the incubator content.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The Digital Twin includes a monitoring component that continuously evaluates the physical twin’s operation against safety criteria. Sensor data (two interior temperature sensors and one exterior sensor) feed calibration and simulation models; violations trigger alerts or corrective actions. While the documents do not detail specific cybersecurity protocols, access‑control mechanisms, or fail‑safe components, the architecture follows general safety‑critical system practices by integrating real‑time monitoring and calibration to maintain safe operation. The open‑source repository on GitHub indicates a transparent implementation but does not specify privacy or consent handling.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
The rest of the report is organized as follows: section 2 introduces the incubator


1 It is worth noting that models can be described at different levels of abstraction, and typically there is a
tendency that accurate models also are very slow to analyse for example by means of simulation.
2 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)


2


Figure 1: Schematic overview of a CPS-based Digital Twins. The letter “C” and “P”
represents “Cyber” and “Physical” respectively.


system, including the hardware and software setup. Then we built two models with
calibration method in section 3. section 4 shows two kinds of experimental results.
Afterwards section 5 sketches the goals of the digital twinning and future work. Finally,
we summarise the report in section 6.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

|Purple line is the
average room
temperature.|Col2|Col3| |---|---|---| ||Orange is Model B|Orange is Model B| Figure 13: The calibration result. approximation of the system magnitude and time scale, whereas the model B is better at predicting the inertia of the heatbed itself. 15 ### **5 Digital Twinning Goals** In this section, we sketch the goals of the digital twinning process for the incubator case study. This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary** We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. Hardware, software, and datasets are available online [6] . Using this example to set the terminology, we proceeded to demystify the DT concept and its goals. 6 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator) 16

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the hardware components and the system's goal based on the text. It correctly identifies the styrofoam box, heatbed, fan, and sensor setup, and the objective to regulate temperature regardless of content. It includes the specific detail that two sensors are inside and one is outside. However, it mentions the 'operational domain is biological sample incubation', which is not explicitly stated in the source text (which only mentions 'thermal incubator' and 'content inside'). This minor unsupported claim prevents a perfect score.

The source text explicitly states in the Introduction that 'Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript', focusing instead on the physical twin and models. While the Introduction mentions general DT capabilities like monitoring and optimization, the specific services listed in the extraction (dashboard, time-series database, Kalman filter, anomaly detection, self-adaptation loop) are not described in the provided text. The extraction relies heavily on information not present in the source document.

The extraction lists specific tools and technologies, including the Raspberry Pi, sensors, GitHub repository, simulation models, and calibration algorithms. It accurately reflects the source descriptions of hardware and software setup, as well as the open-source availability. However, it omits some details like the RabbitMQ server for communication and the specific use of Python Scipy for optimization. Relevant quotes: 'The hardware and software used in this case study are available open source ... publicly available repository.' 'A Raspberry Pi 4 Model B ... was selected to implement the controller.' 'We developed two simulation models ... one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters.' 'A non-linear least squares solver was used to calibrate the parameters ... using an optimisation function inside the python Scipy package.'

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction includes unsupported technical details such as 'Kalman filtering' and 'sub-second responsiveness', as well as a specific 'three-tier temporal architecture' that are not present in the source text. The source only mentions general synchronization and periodic sampling: 'RabbitMQ server was deployed for synchronisation and communication' and 'The low level driver periodically reads the temperature data'.

The extraction accurately describes the architecture as a single, centralized Digital Twin instance with a one-to-one mapping to the physical incubator, which is inferred from the detailed description of the single incubator setup. It correctly identifies the coordination mechanism via the RabbitMQ broker, supported by the quote 'The controller on the Raspberry Pi is listening from the RabbitMQ server' and the description of the communication server.

The description is highly detailed and accurately synthesizes the architecture described in the text. It correctly identifies the three stages (Data ingestion, Model calibration, Monitoring) and specific details like the sensor counts (two internal, one external) and model parameters (Model A with two, Model B with four). This matches the source: 'DT employs calibration algorithms to update the models' and 'resulting data is then used to inform monitors that check whether the system is performing safely'.

While the extraction correctly identifies RabbitMQ as the message broker, it hallucinates the existence of a 'time-series database' and a 'real-time dashboard', which are not mentioned in the source documents. block_3_docs_retrieved:
Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
The rest of the report is organized as follows: section 2 introduces the incubator


1 It is worth noting that models can be described at different levels of abstraction, and typically there is a
tendency that accurate models also are very slow to analyse for example by means of simulation.
2 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)


2


Figure 1: Schematic overview of a CPS-based Digital Twins. The letter “C” and “P”
represents “Cyber” and “Physical” respectively.


system, including the hardware and software setup. Then we built two models with
calibration method in section 3. section 4 shows two kinds of experimental results.
Afterwards section 5 sketches the goals of the digital twinning and future work. Finally,
we summarise the report in section 6.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.


3 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)


3


Insulated

Container







Temperature







Controller


Figure 2: Schematic overview of the Incubator.

|Purple line is the
average room
temperature.|Col2|Col3| |---|---|---| ||Orange is Model B|Orange is Model B| Figure 13: The calibration result. approximation of the system magnitude and time scale, whereas the model B is better at predicting the inertia of the heatbed itself. 15 ### **5 Digital Twinning Goals** In this section, we sketch the goals of the digital twinning process for the incubator case study. This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary** We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. Hardware, software, and datasets are available online [6] . Using this example to set the terminology, we proceeded to demystify the DT concept and its goals. 6 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator) 16

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the two behavioral models (Model A with two parameters, Model B with four) and their differential equation basis. It correctly notes that Model B captures heat-bed thermal inertia. The description of data flow mentions the control bus, whereas the source specifies a 'communication server' (RabbitMQ) for data upload, though the general flow is correct. The mention of 'no external database' is accurate as it is not in the text.

The extraction perfectly aligns with the source descriptions of the heatbed, fan, and Raspberry Pi controller. It correctly identifies the power source (DC supply) and the on-off control strategy. It accurately notes that no external remote command interface is specified in the provided text.

The extraction states that 'The documents do not provide specific sensor models', but the source explicitly names the 'DS18S20 High-Precision 1-Wire Digital Thermometer' in section 2.1.5. This is a direct contradiction to the evidence. Furthermore, the claim about sampling at 'millisecond- to second-intervals' is not supported by the text, which only says 'periodically reads'.

The extraction accurately describes the calibration process using least squares to minimize residual error and correctly identifies that Model B offers higher fidelity by capturing thermal inertia. However, it lists 'sensor noise and variability in heater performance' as sources of uncertainty, which are not explicitly mentioned in the source text (the text mentions 'residual error' and 'discrepancy' but does not attribute causes).

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

**Low-level driver** – Abstracts the low level communication with the sensors and actu
ators.

**Controller** – Implements the control logic required to regulate the temperature inside
the incubator. It receives the measured data and sends commands through the
communication server.


**2.2.1** **The Communication Server**


For the case study, the RabbitMQ [4] server was deployed for synchronisation and communication. With tens of thousands of users, RabbitMQ is one of the most popular
open source message brokers. RabbitMQ is a mature, lightweight, and easy to deploy
on premises and in the cloud.


4 [https://www.rabbitmq.com/](https://www.rabbitmq.com/)


7


Figure 6: Schematic of the software deployment of the components.


The controller on the Raspberry Pi is listening from the RabbitMQ server for commands of controlling the fan and the heatbed and at the same time sharing the control
states to the RabbitMQ server.
We choose RabbitMQ because it greatly facilitates the digital twinning process, as
the DT can be implemented by components that listen to the messages being passed
between the controller and the load level driver, as illustrated in fig. 7.


**2.2.2** **The Low-Level Driver**


The low-level driver is used to interact with the PT and to issue the states of the PT

to the RabbitMQ server. In order keep the device in a safe state, a safe protocol is
incorporated into the low-level driver.
The low level driver periodically reads the temperature data, and checks if there are
commands from the controller, through the communication server. The temperature
data is uploaded to the communication server.


**2.2.3** **The Controller**


The controller used in the case study is a comparable to a bang-bang controller, which
a small difference described below. The control strategy is executed after receiving the

temperature measurements.
The principle of bang-bang control is that when a reference temperature is given
to the controller, the controller issues a heating signal turning on the heatbed until the
measured temperature from sensors reaches the reference temperature. Because of the
delayed effect of the temperature, the controller needs to wait after each actuation, to
make sure the temperature does not rise too much. Figure 8 shows the state chart of the
controller. The controller parameters are:


8


|Physical Twin|Digital Twin|
|---|---|
|||



Figure 7: Overview of communication in the Incubator. The arrows represent the information flow.


**LL** – Lower limit for temperature;
**UL** – Upper limit for temperature;
**H** – Heating duration;
**C** – Waiting duration.















Figure 8: Controller Statechart.

### **3 Modelling the Physical Twin**


The dynamics model we created mainly focuses on predicting the temperature inside
the styrofoam box. To simplify the model, a few assumptions were made.


9

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the low-level driver on the Raspberry Pi, the periodic reading of temperature data from internal and external sensors, and the transmission of this data to the RabbitMQ server. It accurately captures the flow and the presence of a safe protocol. However, it adds unsupported details such as the specific message format ('timestamped value'), the protocol 'AMQP TCP', and the specific logic of the safe protocol ('validates sensor ranges'), which are not found in the source text.

The description accurately details the control flow, noting that the controller receives commands from RabbitMQ, uses bang-bang logic, and shares states back to the broker. It correctly identifies that the low-level driver executes commands and incorporates a safe protocol. The flow 'via the same broker' is supported by the schematic and text references to the communication server. Quotes: 'The controller on the Raspberry Pi is listening from the RabbitMQ server for commands of controlling the fan and the heatbed and at the same time sharing the control states to the RabbitMQ server.'; 'The controller used in the case study is a comparable to a bang-bang controller... issues a heating signal turning on the heatbed.'; 'In order keep the device in a safe state, a safe protocol is incorporated into the low-level driver.'

The extraction accurately describes the system's use of RabbitMQ as the central open-source message broker and the publish/subscribe pattern used by the components. It correctly states the on-premises deployment capability. It loses points for inferring specific technical protocols ('AMQP', 'TCP') and performance characteristics ('near-real-time') that are not explicitly stated in the document, though the architectural summary is strong.

The description is highly accurate and well-supported by the text. It correctly states that the RabbitMQ server and controller run on the same Raspberry Pi, accurately describing the on-premises nature of the deployment.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
The rest of the report is organized as follows: section 2 introduces the incubator

system, including the hardware and software setup. Then we built two models with
calibration method in section 3. section 4 shows two kinds of experimental results.
Afterwards section 5 sketches the goals of the digital twinning and future work. Finally,
we summarise the report in section 6.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with
